point_001: The motivation is poorly defined: the paper conflates two different goals (improving OOD performance of full fine-tuning vs. improving ID performance of parameter-efficient tuning) without clearly committing to either, and fails to compare against existing OOD generalization or domain adaptation methods.
point_002: The ensemble method is not a novel contribution — applying ensembles to combine full fine-tuning and lightweight fine-tuning is a straightforward application of an existing technique, yet the paper claims it as a proposed method.
point_003: The cocktail fine-tuning method only performs comparably to the ensemble baseline rather than consistently and significantly surpassing it, weakening the case for the proposed approach.
point_004: The computational cost of the distillation step in cocktail fine-tuning (requiring inference over the entire training set using the adapter model) is not discussed as a limitation.
point_005: Hyperparameter choices for parameter-efficient tuning methods (e.g., prefix length, bottleneck dimension) are not justified; with properly tuned hyperparameters, lightweight fine-tuning might match full fine-tuning on ID data, undermining the paper's premise.
point_006: The claimed trade-off between ID and OOD performance across fine-tuning schemes is task-dependent and not universal: for Data2Text, full fine-tuning and lightweight fine-tuning show nearly identical ID scores, and for summarization OOD scores are also similar. Reporting only averages obscures these important differences.
point_007: Evaluation relies heavily on BLEU and ROUGE-2, metrics with well-documented low validity for NLG tasks; no alternative or human evaluation is provided, making results difficult to interpret reliably.
point_008: The definitions of in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) splits are insufficiently described, and the number of examples used for each split is not reported.
point_009: The paper lacks experiments on machine translation, a major NLG task, limiting the generalizability of the conclusions.
point_010: The description of prefix-tuning is technically inaccurate: the paper states it prepends trainable vectors to the input, whereas prefix-tuning actually prepends them to the projected key and value vectors in the attention layers.
point_011: The reported ROUGE-2 score on XSUM for the full fine-tuning baseline is lower than the originally published BART result, with no explanation provided for this discrepancy.
point_012: The proposed ensemble approach eliminates the parameter-efficiency advantage of lightweight fine-tuning (since both models must be stored and run), yet this trade-off is not acknowledged or discussed.
